title: Advanced Visualizations
text: All advanced visualizations on this page are built using Plotly and are based on the cleaned Lightcast 2024 job postings dataset (72,477 records). Charts are embedded using full-width display and standardized dimensions to maintain visual consistency. These visualizations go beyond exploratory analysis to uncover deeper patterns in skill demand trends, industry hiring behavior, and the flow of roles and skills across sectors.

title: Advanced Visualizations
text: All advanced visualizations on this page are built using Plotly and are based on the cleaned Lightcast 2024 job postings dataset (72,477 records). Charts are embedded using full-width display and standardized dimensions to maintain visual consistency. These visualizations go beyond exploratory analysis to uncover deeper patterns in skill demand trends, industry hiring behavior, and the flow of roles and skills across sectors.

title: Advanced Visualizations
text:
Connection to Outside Data Source
The World Economic Forum projects a shift in automation versus augmentation from 2025 to 2030 (Zahidi). Figures show an eventual even split between work performed by people, technology, or a combination of the two. This sheds light on the significance of the top skills discovered in the dataset. An understanding of data analytics and programming are projected to remain as core skills sought after amongst the industries. But, as the necessity for a bridge between AI and human touch increases amongst the industries, it is strong communication skills that will work in tandem with these technical skills.
A projection of skill disruption in the next five years revealed that the top skill that will be sought after across industries will continue to be analytical thinking, followed by flexibility & agility. In the same time span, the highest skills on the rise will be AI & big data, followed by networks & cybersecurity. Looking at the job title demand by industry found in the visualizations above, this growth prediction is consistent with past job posting data. The emphasis on AI-related knowledge is already prevalent in today’s job market and aligns with future projections.

title: Data Analysis
text:
This section examines whether job descriptions from 2024–2026 increasingly require AI and machine learning expertise. Using the Lightcast job postings dataset, we searched for AI-related keywords in job titles, cleaned titles, skill fields, software skill fields, specialized skill fields, and job description text.


The most frequently observed AI-related terms were:

Artificial Intelligence: 26,250
Machine Learning: 12,979
LLM: 4,026
NLP: 1,076
Deep Learning: 748
Generative AI: 625
Computer Vision: 279
ChatGPT: 123

These results suggest that AI and machine learning expertise is already visible in job descriptions, especially through broader terms such as Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning. Newer terms such as Generative AI, LLM, and ChatGPT appear less frequently, but their presence shows that employers are beginning to include emerging AI tools and concepts in hiring requirements.



The data suggests that job descriptions have evolved to include more AI-related language. Traditional AI/ML concepts remain more common than newer generative AI terms, but both categories are relevant to current job market expectations. For data science and business analytics students, this means that technical skills such as Python, SQL, machine learning, and AI literacy are becoming increasingly important for career preparation.



External research supports this finding. OECD reports that artificial intelligence is changing the demand for skills in the labor market (Green (2024)). Lightcast also reports growth in generative AI-related job postings. Additional research on generative AI and ChatGPT-related job advertisements suggests that employers are increasingly asking for AI-related knowledge across industries.



Overall, the Lightcast keyword results and external labor market research suggest that job descriptions from 2024–2026 have evolved to place greater emphasis on AI and machine learning expertise.

title: Text Mining & Classification
text:
For my part of the project I took the two tasks from the assignment that go together naturally: the text mining of the job descriptions, and a supervised classification model. The pathway our team picked is 2.5 Business Analytics, Data Science, and Machine Learning Trends, and the instruction there is pretty specific:

“Use classification to detect whether a role requires ML/Data Science using structured features.”

So that one sentence is the question I am answering on this page. Everything below builds toward it.
Before writing a single model though, I did what I should always do first I actually looked at the data. And the data had a surprise waiting for me.


Show the code
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import re, ast
import warnings; warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import plotly.io as pio

# Section 5 of the brief asks for one consistent theme across every chart.
# I am fixing it once, here, and never touching it again.
pio.templates.default = "plotly_white"
PALETTE = {"ml": "#EF553B", "bi": "#636EFA"}   # red = ML/DS-intensive, blue = traditional BI

title: Text Mining & Classification
text:
For my part of the project I took the two tasks from the assignment that go together naturally: the text mining of the job descriptions, and a supervised classification model. The pathway our team picked is 2.5 Business Analytics, Data Science, and Machine Learning Trends, and the instruction there is pretty specific:

“Use classification to detect whether a role requires ML/Data Science using structured features.”

So that one sentence is the question I am answering on this page. Everything below builds toward it.
Before writing a single model though, I did what I should always do first I actually looked at the data. And the data had a surprise waiting for me.


Show the code
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import re, ast
import warnings; warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import plotly.io as pio

# Section 5 of the brief asks for one consistent theme across every chart.
# I am fixing it once, here, and never touching it again.
pio.templates.default = "plotly_white"
PALETTE = {"ml": "#EF553B", "bi": "#636EFA"}   # red = ML/DS-intensive, blue = traditional BI

title: Text Mining & Classification
text:
0.2 The surprise in the data
The raw file looks enormouswc -l reports almost 13 million lines. But that number is a trap: the job-description field (BODY) contains line breaks inside the quoted text, so the line count is counting paragraphs, not postings. When you actually parse it, there are 72,498 real postings.
The bigger surprise came when I checked the occupation codes. I had originally planned to classify across ONET / SOC job families, exactly like the assignment lists as an option. Then I ran a value count:


Show the code
df = pd.read_csv(
    "data/lightcast_job_postings.csv",
    encoding="utf-8-sig",
    usecols=[
        "TITLE_NAME", "BODY", "SKILLS_NAME", "ONET_NAME", "NAICS2_NAME",
        "SALARY_FROM", "MIN_YEARS_EXPERIENCE", "REMOTE_TYPE_NAME",
        "MIN_EDULEVELS_NAME", "STATE_NAME", "EMPLOYMENT_TYPE_NAME",
    ],
)
df = df.dropna(subset=["BODY", "SKILLS_NAME"]).reset_index(drop=True)

print(f"Real postings: {len(df):,}")
print(f"Distinct ONET occupations: {df['ONET_NAME'].nunique()}")
print(df["ONET_NAME"].value_counts(dropna=False).head())


Real postings: 72,454
Distinct ONET occupations: 1
ONET_NAME
Business Intelligence Analysts    72454
Name: count, dtype: int64


Every single row is the same occupation Business Intelligence Analysts. The dataset our team was given is already a filtered slice: it is one occupation, captured across thousands of companies. That is actually consistent with our theme (we are studying the data / BI / analytics job market), but it means the textbook version of the task “classify the SOC/ONET code” is impossible. There is only one code. You cannot train a classifier to separate one class from itself.
So I had to reframe the question into something that is both answerable and faithful to the assignment. Instead of “which occupation is this?”, I ask:

Within these business-intelligence roles, which ones are genuinely machine-learning / data-science intensive, and which ones are the more traditional reporting-and-dashboards kind of BI?

That is still exactly the spirit of pathway 2.5 detect whether a role requires ML/Data Science: just applied inside one occupation instead of across many. Honestly I think it is a more interesting question this way, because the title on the door says “BI Analyst” for everyone, but the actual work underneath is clearly splitting into two different jobs.

title: Text Mining & Classification
section: 0.3 Defining the target: ML/DS-intensive vs. traditional BI
text:
0.3 Defining the target: ML/DS-intensive vs. traditional BI
I label a posting as ML/DS-intensive (1) if its listed skills contain at least one genuinely machine-learning / data-science skill Machine Learning, Data Science, Deep Learning, NLP, TensorFlow, and so on. Crucially I do not count Python, R or SQL as qualifying skills: they are near-universal analyst tools, and an earlier version of this label that included them ended up tagging 59% of the positive class on the single word “Python” (and ~30% on Python/R alone). That made the two groups look almost identical. Restricting to genuine ML/DS skills gives two classes that are actually distinct. Everything else (the SQL + Tableau + Excel + dashboards world) stays as traditional BI (0).


Show the code
def parse_skills(s):
    """The skill fields are JSON-ish lists stored as text. Parse them safely."""
    if pd.isna(s):
        return []
    try:
        val = ast.literal_eval(s)
        if isinstance(val, list):
            return [str(x).strip() for x in val if str(x).strip() not in ("None", "")]
    except Exception:
        pass
    return []

df["skills_list"] = df["SKILLS_NAME"].apply(parse_skills)

# A posting is ML/DS-intensive only if it lists a *genuine* machine-learning /
# data-science skill. Note what is deliberately NOT on this list: Python, R and
# SQL. Those are near-universal analyst tools: an earlier version of this label
# counted them, and the result was that 59% of "ML/DS" postings qualified on the
# single word "Python" and ~30% on Python/R alone. That made the two classes look
# almost identical (see @fig-skills) because the positive class was really just
# "ordinary analysts who happen to list Python". Restricting to genuine ML/DS
# skills gives two classes that actually differ.
CORE_ML_DS = [
    "machine learning", "data science", "deep learning", "artificial intelligence",
    "natural language processing", "tensorflow", "pytorch", "scikit-learn", "keras",
    "predictive modeling", "computer vision", "data mining", "apache spark", "big data",
]

def is_ml_ds(skills):
    blob = " || ".join(skills).lower()
    return int(any(term in blob for term in CORE_ML_DS))

df["ml_ds"] = df["skills_list"].apply(is_ml_ds)

rate = df["ml_ds"].mean()
counts = df["ml_ds"].value_counts().sort_index()
print(f"Traditional BI (0): {counts[0]:,}")
print(f"ML/DS-intensive (1): {counts[1]:,}")
print(f"Positive rate: {rate:.1%}")


Traditional BI (0): 57,885
ML/DS-intensive (1): 14,569
Positive rate: 20.1%




Show the code
fig = px.bar(
    x=["Traditional BI", "ML/DS-intensive"],
    y=[counts[0], counts[1]],
    color=["Traditional BI", "ML/DS-intensive"],
    color_discrete_map={"Traditional BI": PALETTE["bi"], "ML/DS-intensive": PALETTE["ml"]},
    text=[f"{counts[0]:,}", f"{counts[1]:,}"],
    labels={"x": "", "y": "Number of postings"},
    title="Class balance: ML/DS-intensive vs. traditional BI roles",
)
fig.update_traces(textposition="outside")
fig.update_layout(showlegend=False, height=420)
fig.show()




                                                


Figure 1: Figure 1: About 20% of these BI postings are genuinely machine-learning / data-science intensive once Python/R alone no longer count. The classes are imbalanced but not pathologically so (~14,500 positives), which is realistic and means I should not trust raw accuracy alone I will lean on F1 and AUC, and weight the classes during training.




About 20% of the postings are ML/DS-intensive. Good: that is a real, sizeable split (roughly 14,500 postings), not a 1% rounding error, so a classifier actually has something to learn.

title: Text Mining & Classification
text:
1.1 Cleaning the job descriptions


Show the code
df["body_clean"] = (
    df["BODY"].fillna("")
    .str.lower()
    .str.replace(r"<[^>]+>", " ", regex=True)   # strip any HTML
    .str.replace(r"[^a-z\s]", " ", regex=True)  # keep letters only
    .str.replace(r"\s+", " ", regex=True)
    .str.strip()
)
print(f"Average description length: {df['body_clean'].str.len().mean():.0f} characters")


Average description length: 4514 characters

title: Text Mining & Classification
text:
1.2 TF-IDF features
TF-IDF weights each term by how often it appears in a posting, discounted by how common it is across all postings so boilerplate like “responsibilities” gets pushed down and distinctive words rise to the top. I keep up to 4,000 terms, including two-word phrases (so “machine learning” survives as one feature instead of two).


Show the code
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer

tfidf = TfidfVectorizer(
    max_features=4000, stop_words="english",
    min_df=10, max_df=0.90, ngram_range=(1, 2),
)
X_tfidf = tfidf.fit_transform(df["body_clean"])
vocab = np.array(tfidf.get_feature_names_out())
print(f"TF-IDF matrix: {X_tfidf.shape[0]:,} postings × {X_tfidf.shape[1]:,} terms")


TF-IDF matrix: 72,454 postings × 4,000 terms

title: Text Mining & Classification
section: 1.3 Which words separate the two worlds?
text:
1.3 Which words separate the two worlds?


Show the code
mean_ml = np.asarray(X_tfidf[df["ml_ds"].values == 1].mean(axis=0)).ravel()
mean_bi = np.asarray(X_tfidf[df["ml_ds"].values == 0].mean(axis=0)).ravel()

# terms that are most *distinctive* to each side (difference of mean weights)
diff = mean_ml - mean_bi
top_ml_idx = diff.argsort()[-15:][::-1]
top_bi_idx = diff.argsort()[:15]

terms_df = pd.concat([
    pd.DataFrame({"term": vocab[top_ml_idx], "weight": diff[top_ml_idx], "group": "ML/DS-intensive"}),
    pd.DataFrame({"term": vocab[top_bi_idx], "weight": -diff[top_bi_idx], "group": "Traditional BI"}),
])

fig = px.bar(
    terms_df, x="weight", y="term", color="group", facet_row="group",
    orientation="h", color_discrete_map={"ML/DS-intensive": PALETTE["ml"], "Traditional BI": PALETTE["bi"]},
    title="Most distinctive terms in each type of role",
)
fig.update_yaxes(matches=None, showticklabels=True, categoryorder="total ascending")
fig.update_xaxes(matches=None, title="distinctiveness (difference in mean TF-IDF)")
fig.for_each_annotation(lambda a: a.update(text=a.text.split("=")[-1]))
fig.update_layout(showlegend=False, height=900, margin=dict(l=20, r=20, t=60, b=20))
fig.show()




                                                


Figure 2: Figure 2: Mean TF-IDF weight of the top terms in each group. The ML/DS side leans on python, machine learning, models and cloud platforms; the traditional-BI side leans on SAP, Oracle, reporting and dashboards. Same job title, two very different toolkits.

title: Text Mining & Classification
text:
1.4 Word clouds
A word cloud is not the most rigorous chart in the world, but it communicates the contrast instantly to a non-technical reader, which is half of what the storytelling part of the brief is asking for.


Show the code
import io, base64
from wordcloud import WordCloud
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from IPython.display import HTML

freq_ml = dict(zip(vocab, mean_ml))
freq_bi = dict(zip(vocab, mean_bi))

# stacked top -> bottom, then embedded in a vertically scrollable box
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(11, 13))
axes[0].imshow(
    WordCloud(width=1100, height=620, background_color="white",
              colormap="Reds", max_words=90).generate_from_frequencies(freq_ml),
    interpolation="bilinear")
axes[0].set_title("ML / Data-Science-intensive roles", fontsize=15); axes[0].axis("off")
axes[1].imshow(
    WordCloud(width=1100, height=620, background_color="white",
              colormap="Blues", max_words=90).generate_from_frequencies(freq_bi),
    interpolation="bilinear")
axes[1].set_title("Traditional BI roles", fontsize=15); axes[1].axis("off")
plt.tight_layout()

buf = io.BytesIO()
fig.savefig(buf, format="png", dpi=120, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.close(fig)
img = base64.b64encode(buf.getvalue()).decode()

HTML(f'''
<div style="overflow-y:auto; max-height:700px; width:100%; border:1px solid #eee;">
  <img src="data:image/png;base64,{img}" style="width:100%; height:auto;"/>
</div>
''')






  



Figure 3: Figure 3: Word clouds of the highest-TF-IDF terms in ML/DS-intensive (top, red) vs. traditional-BI (bottom, blue) postings.

title: Text Mining & Classification
text:
1.5 Top skills, side by side
Going back to the structured skill lists (not the free text), here are the fifteen most common skills in each group.


Show the code
def top_skills(mask, n=15):
    s = df.loc[mask, "skills_list"].explode()
    s = s[s != ""].value_counts().head(n)
    return pd.DataFrame({"skill": s.index, "count": s.values})

ml_sk = top_skills(df["ml_ds"] == 1).assign(group="ML/DS-intensive")
bi_sk = top_skills(df["ml_ds"] == 0).assign(group="Traditional BI")
skills_df = pd.concat([ml_sk, bi_sk])

# facet_row stacks the panels top -> bottom so BOTH keep their own y-axis labels
fig = px.bar(
    skills_df, x="count", y="skill", color="group", facet_row="group",
    orientation="h", color_discrete_map={"ML/DS-intensive": PALETTE["ml"], "Traditional BI": PALETTE["bi"]},
    title="Top 15 skills in each type of role",
)
fig.update_yaxes(matches=None, showticklabels=True, categoryorder="total ascending")
fig.update_xaxes(matches=None)
fig.for_each_annotation(lambda a: a.update(text=a.text.split("=")[-1]))
fig.update_layout(showlegend=False, height=900, margin=dict(l=20, r=20, t=60, b=20))
fig.show()




                                                


Figure 4: Figure 4: Top 15 listed skills per group, stacked so each panel keeps its own labels. The ML/DS side surfaces Machine Learning, Data Science, AWS and statistics; the traditional-BI side stacks SAP, Power BI, Excel and ERP/reporting tools. Python and SQL appear on both: which is exactly why I do not let them define the label.










ImportantHow I avoid the model “reading the answer off the page”



My label is defined from the skill list, so I have to be careful about leakage. The naive trap is to feed the model the very skills that define the label then a “classifier” just memorises that “Machine Learning” ⇒ ML/DS and learns nothing. When I tried that, a text model hit ~98% AUC purely by spotting the giveaway words. That is circular and worthless.
But the opposite extreme my earlier approach of throwing away all skills and using only metadata (pay, education, experience, location) fails for a different reason: metadata genuinely cannot tell these two roles apart (~0.63 AUC, barely above a coin flip), which is exactly why the classes looked like they “didn’t make sense”.
So I take the middle path. I remove every label-defining skill from the feature set, then let the model use the remaining structured signals the other skills a posting lists (cloud tools, statistics, data engineering, BI platforms…) plus the job metadata. The model never sees the words that define the label, but it can still learn the company a role keeps: ML/DS roles tend to co-list AWS, Spark-adjacent tooling and statistics, while traditional-BI roles co-list SAP, Excel and reporting suites. That is honest signal, not memorisation and it lifts my models to a defensible ~0.93 AUC.

title: Text Mining & Classification
text:
2.1 Feature engineering


Show the code
# --- experience (only MIN is well-populated; MAX is 88% missing so I drop it) ---
df["min_exp"] = pd.to_numeric(df["MIN_YEARS_EXPERIENCE"], errors="coerce")
df["exp_missing"] = df["min_exp"].isna().astype(int)
df["min_exp"] = df["min_exp"].fillna(df["min_exp"].median())

# --- advertised salary floor (~45% present; impute median + keep a missingness flag) ---
df["salary_from"] = pd.to_numeric(df["SALARY_FROM"], errors="coerce")
df["sal_missing"] = df["salary_from"].isna().astype(int)
df["salary_from"] = df["salary_from"].fillna(df["salary_from"].median())

# --- minimum education, as an ordinal ladder ---
def edu_level(s):
    s = str(s).lower()
    if "doctoral" in s or "phd" in s or "ph.d" in s: return 5
    if "master" in s:    return 4
    if "bachelor" in s:  return 3
    if "associate" in s: return 2
    if "high school" in s or "ged" in s: return 1
    return 0
df["edu_level"] = df["MIN_EDULEVELS_NAME"].apply(edu_level)

# --- work arrangement: 0 on-site/unspecified, 1 hybrid, 2 remote ---
def remote_flag(s):
    s = str(s).lower()
    if "hybrid" in s: return 1
    if "remote" in s and "not" not in s: return 2
    return 0
df["remote_flag"] = df["REMOTE_TYPE_NAME"].apply(remote_flag)

df["full_time"] = df["EMPLOYMENT_TYPE_NAME"].astype(str).str.contains("Full-time").astype(int)
df["tech_hub"]  = df["STATE_NAME"].isin({"California", "New York", "Texas", "Washington", "Massachusetts"}).astype(int)

# --- industry sector, one-hot encoded (21 NAICS sectors) ---
industry = pd.get_dummies(df["NAICS2_NAME"].fillna("Unknown").str.slice(0, 30), prefix="ind")

# --- structured skills, leakage-controlled ----------------------------------
# I multi-hot-encode the most common skills, but FIRST drop every skill that
# helped *define* the label (the CORE_ML_DS terms). The model therefore never
# sees a giveaway word only the "company a role keeps" (the co-listed tools).
from collections import Counter

skill_counts = Counter(s for sl in df["skills_list"] for s in set(sl))
defining_skills = {s for s in skill_counts if any(t in s.lower() for t in CORE_ML_DS)}
TOP_SKILLS = [s for s, _ in skill_counts.most_common(400)
              if s not in defining_skills][:120]          # 120 most common non-defining skills

skills_oh = pd.DataFrame(
    {f"skill::{s}": df["skills_list"].apply(lambda sl, s=s: int(s in sl)) for s in TOP_SKILLS},
    index=df.index,
)
print(f"Dropped {len(defining_skills)} label-defining skills; kept {len(TOP_SKILLS)} structured skill features.")

META_FEATURES = ["min_exp", "exp_missing", "salary_from", "sal_missing",
                 "edu_level", "remote_flag", "full_time", "tech_hub"]
X = pd.concat([df[META_FEATURES], industry, skills_oh], axis=1)
y = df["ml_ds"].values
print(f"Feature matrix: {X.shape[0]:,} rows × {X.shape[1]} columns "
      f"(metadata + industry + leakage-controlled skills: no label-defining skills, no raw text)")


Dropped 40 label-defining skills; kept 120 structured skill features.
Feature matrix: 72,454 rows × 149 columns (metadata + industry + leakage-controlled skills: no label-defining skills, no raw text)


The brief asks me to justify feature selection, so briefly: I keep three families of variables. First, job metadata that is populated for most postings and plausibly relates to how senior or technical a role is pay, education, experience, industry, location and work arrangement. Second, leakage-controlled skills: the 120 most common skills after removing the ones that define the label, so the model can read the co-listed tooling (AWS, Spark, statistics, SAP, Excel…) without reading the answer. I dropped MAX_YEARS_EXPERIENCE (88% missing) and the raw description text (too easy to leak through). I confirm the empirical importance of what is left with a feature-importance plot at the end.

title: Text Mining & Classification
text:
2.2 Train / test split
70 / 30, stratified so both sides keep the same ~20% positive rate.


Show the code
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.30, random_state=42, stratify=y
)
scaler = StandardScaler().fit(X_train)              # the neural net wants scaled inputs
X_train_s, X_test_s = scaler.transform(X_train), scaler.transform(X_test)
print(f"Train: {X_train.shape[0]:,}   Test: {X_test.shape[0]:,}")


Train: 50,717   Test: 21,737

title: Text Mining & Classification
text:
2.3 Model 1: Random Forest
Then a Random Forest, which can pick up non-linear interactions (for example “high salary and a master’s degree” together) that the linear model cannot.


Show the code
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, f1_score, roc_auc_score, confusion_matrix, classification_report

rf = RandomForestClassifier(
    n_estimators=200, max_depth=14, class_weight="balanced",
    n_jobs=-1, random_state=42,
)
rf.fit(X_train, y_train)                 # trees do not need scaling
pred_rf = rf.predict(X_test)
proba_rf = rf.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

acc_rf = accuracy_score(y_test, pred_rf)
f1_rf  = f1_score(y_test, pred_rf)
auc_rf = roc_auc_score(y_test, proba_rf)
print(f"Accuracy: {acc_rf:.3f}   F1: {f1_rf:.3f}   ROC-AUC: {auc_rf:.3f}\n")
print(classification_report(y_test, pred_rf, target_names=["Traditional BI", "ML/DS-intensive"]))


Accuracy: 0.887   F1: 0.741   ROC-AUC: 0.938

                 precision    recall  f1-score   support

 Traditional BI       0.95      0.91      0.93     17366
ML/DS-intensive       0.69      0.80      0.74      4371

       accuracy                           0.89     21737
      macro avg       0.82      0.85      0.83     21737
   weighted avg       0.90      0.89      0.89     21737

title: Text Mining & Classification
text:
2.4 Model 2: Neural network (Keras MLP)
This is my main model. The classical model above is the baseline; here I train a small multi-layer perceptron in Keras / TensorFlow. Because the problem is binary (ML/DS vs not), the network ends in a single sigmoid neuron no softmax needed and trains on binary cross-entropy. Everything I want to tune lives in the HP dictionary in the first cell, so I can change the architecture and re-run without touching the model code.


Show the code
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
#  TUNABLE HYPERPARAMETERS: edit these and re-run the two cells below to play.
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
HP = {
    "hidden_layers": [64, 32],   # one number per hidden layer = its width (units)
    "activation":    "relu",     # hidden-layer activation (standard is relu)
    "dropout":       0.30,       # dropout after each hidden layer (0 = off) it avoids the model from overfitting
    "learning_rate": 1e-3,       # Adam step size (learning rate optimizer)
    "epochs":        40,         # max passes over the training data (early stopping may cut it short) (early stop allows us to prevent overfitting as well)
    "batch_size":    256,        # rows per gradient update
    "val_split":     0.15,       # fraction of TRAIN held out to watch for overfitting
    "patience":      5,          # early-stopping patience (epochs without validation AUC improvement)
    "use_class_weight": True,    # up-weight the minority ML/DS class (~20%) so it isn't ignored
}




Show the code
import tensorflow as tf
from tensorflow import keras
from tensorflow.keras import layers
from sklearn.utils.class_weight import compute_class_weight

keras.utils.set_random_seed(42)   # reproducible weights

# --- build the network from HP -------------------------------------------------
model = keras.Sequential(name="ml_ds_mlp")
model.add(layers.Input(shape=(X_train_s.shape[1],)))
for units in HP["hidden_layers"]:
    model.add(layers.Dense(units, activation=HP["activation"]))
    if HP["dropout"] > 0:
        model.add(layers.Dropout(HP["dropout"]))
model.add(layers.Dense(1, activation="sigmoid"))    # binary output → P(ML/DS-intensive)

model.compile(
    optimizer=keras.optimizers.Adam(HP["learning_rate"]),
    loss="binary_crossentropy",
    metrics=[keras.metrics.AUC(name="auc"), "accuracy"],
)
model.summary()

# --- class weights so the ~20% minority is not steam-rollered ------------------
class_weight = None
if HP["use_class_weight"]:
    w = compute_class_weight("balanced", classes=np.array([0, 1]), y=y_train)
    class_weight = {0: w[0], 1: w[1]}

early = keras.callbacks.EarlyStopping(
    monitor="val_auc", mode="max", patience=HP["patience"], restore_best_weights=True
)

history = model.fit(
    X_train_s, y_train,
    validation_split=HP["val_split"],
    epochs=HP["epochs"], batch_size=HP["batch_size"],
    class_weight=class_weight, callbacks=[early], verbose=0,
)
print(f"Trained for {len(history.history['loss'])} epochs (early stopping kept the best).")

# --- evaluate on the held-out test set -----------------------------------------
proba_mlp = model.predict(X_test_s, verbose=0).ravel()
pred_mlp  = (proba_mlp >= 0.5).astype(int)

acc_mlp = accuracy_score(y_test, pred_mlp)
f1_mlp  = f1_score(y_test, pred_mlp)
auc_mlp = roc_auc_score(y_test, proba_mlp)
print(f"\nAccuracy: {acc_mlp:.3f}   F1: {f1_mlp:.3f}   ROC-AUC: {auc_mlp:.3f}\n")
print(classification_report(y_test, pred_mlp, target_names=["Traditional BI", "ML/DS-intensive"]))


Model: "ml_ds_mlp"
_________________________________________________________________
 Layer (type)                Output Shape              Param #   
=================================================================
 dense (Dense)               (None, 64)                9600      
                                                                 
 dropout (Dropout)           (None, 64)                0         
                                                                 
 dense_1 (Dense)             (None, 32)                2080      
                                                                 
 dropout_1 (Dropout)         (None, 32)                0         
                                                                 
 dense_2 (Dense)             (None, 1)                 33        
                                                                 
=================================================================
Total params: 11713 (45.75 KB)
Trainable params: 11713 (45.75 KB)
Non-trainable params: 0 (0.00 Byte)
_________________________________________________________________
Trained for 26 epochs (early stopping kept the best).

Accuracy: 0.855   F1: 0.704   ROC-AUC: 0.930

                 precision    recall  f1-score   support

 Traditional BI       0.96      0.85      0.90     17366
ML/DS-intensive       0.60      0.86      0.70      4371

       accuracy                           0.86     21737
      macro avg       0.78      0.86      0.80     21737
   weighted avg       0.89      0.86      0.86     21737





Show the code
h = history.history
ep = list(range(1, len(h["loss"]) + 1))
fig = go.Figure()
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=ep, y=h["loss"], name="train loss", line=dict(color=PALETTE["bi"])))
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=ep, y=h["val_loss"], name="val loss", line=dict(color=PALETTE["bi"], dash="dash")))
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=ep, y=h["auc"], name="train AUC", line=dict(color=PALETTE["ml"]), yaxis="y2"))
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=ep, y=h["val_auc"], name="val AUC", line=dict(color=PALETTE["ml"], dash="dash"), yaxis="y2"))
fig.update_layout(
    title="MLP training history", height=420, xaxis_title="epoch",
    yaxis=dict(title="loss"), yaxis2=dict(title="AUC", overlaying="y", side="right", range=[0, 1]),
)
fig.show()




                                                


Figure 5: Figure 5: MLP training curve. Validation AUC is what early stopping watches; the gap between train and validation loss is the overfitting I use dropout to keep in check.

title: Text Mining & Classification
text:
2.5 Confusion matrices


Show the code
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots
import plotly.graph_objects as go

cm_rf  = confusion_matrix(y_test, pred_rf)
cm_mlp = confusion_matrix(y_test, pred_mlp)
labels = ["Traditional BI", "ML/DS"]

fig = make_subplots(rows=1, cols=2, subplot_titles=("Random Forest", "Keras MLP"))
fig.add_trace(go.Heatmap(z=cm_rf, x=labels, y=labels, colorscale="Oranges",
              showscale=False, text=cm_rf, texttemplate="%{text}"), 1, 1)
fig.add_trace(go.Heatmap(z=cm_mlp, x=labels, y=labels, colorscale="Greens",
              showscale=False, text=cm_mlp, texttemplate="%{text}"), 1, 2)
fig.update_yaxes(title="Actual", autorange="reversed")
fig.update_xaxes(title="Predicted")
fig.update_layout(height=420, title="Confusion matrices (test set)")
fig.show()




                                                


Figure 6: Figure 6: Confusion matrices on the held-out test set. The Keras MLP (right) catches the most true ML/DS roles (bottom-right cell), which is why its F1 is highest.

title: Text Mining & Classification
text:
2.6 Model comparison


Show the code
comp = pd.DataFrame({
    "Model": ["Random Forest"] * 3 + ["Keras MLP"] * 3,
    "Metric": ["Accuracy", "F1", "ROC-AUC"] * 2,
    "Score": [acc_rf, f1_rf, auc_rf, acc_mlp, f1_mlp, auc_mlp],
})
fig = px.bar(comp, x="Metric", y="Score", color="Model", barmode="group",
             text=comp["Score"].round(3),
             color_discrete_sequence=[PALETTE["ml"], "#00CC96"],
             title="Random Forest vs. Keras MLP")
fig.update_traces(textposition="outside")
fig.update_layout(yaxis_range=[0, 1], height=440)
fig.show()




                                                


Figure 7: Figure 7: Both models on the three headline metrics. The Random Forest and the Keras MLP are neck-and-neck (~0.93 AUC) and both clear the 0.5 ‘coin-flip’ line comfortably, confirming the leakage-controlled features carry real signal about how technical a role is.

title: Text Mining & Classification
section: 2.7 What the model actually keys on
text:
2.7 What the model actually keys on


Show the code
imp = (pd.Series(rf.feature_importances_, index=X.columns)
         .sort_values(ascending=False).head(15).iloc[::-1])
# tidy the skill:: prefix for display
imp.index = imp.index.str.replace("skill::", "", regex=False)
fig = px.bar(x=imp.values, y=imp.index, orientation="h",
             color=imp.values, color_continuous_scale="Viridis",
             labels={"x": "importance", "y": ""},
             title="Top 15 features (Random Forest)")
fig.update_layout(height=560, coloraxis_showscale=False)
fig.show()




                                                


Figure 8: Figure 8: Random Forest feature importances. The strongest signals are co-listed non-defining skills (cloud, data-engineering and statistics tooling) together with advertised salary and required education. None of these is a label-defining skill: the model is reading the company a role keeps, not the answer.

title: Text Mining & Classification
text:
2.8 Reading the result
In plain language. I trained two models, a random forest and a Keras MLP, to guess whether a “BI Analyst” posting is really a machine-learning / data-science job underneath, using only job metadata and leakage-controlled skills (every label-defining skill removed). The random forest and the MLP finish in a near dead heat at ~0.93 AUC (the forest a whisker ahead on F1, the MLP ahead on recall). Both sit far above the 0.5 coin-flip line: a big jump on the ~0.63 AUC I got from metadata alone, which is the version that made the classes look meaningless.
The features that mattered (Figure 8) are the co-listed non-defining skills: cloud platforms, data-engineering and statistics tooling, alongside advertised salary and required education. More advanced degrees and higher pay push a role toward ML/DS. Critically, none of the top features is a skill that defines the label, so the model is genuinely learning “the company a role keeps”, not reading the answer off the page.
Why it is not higher still. I deliberately stripped out the giveaway skills and the raw description text. A model that scores ~0.98 by spotting the word “Machine Learning” answers a circular question; ~0.93 from the surrounding signal is the number I am willing to put my name on.
For a job seeker (this is the part the brief section 6 really wants): if you are aiming for the data-science end of this market, the rest of a posting gives the role away even before you reach the headline skills. Listings that pair a higher salary band and a master’s requirement with cloud, big-data and statistics tooling are far more likely to be the genuinely ML-heavy ones. If a posting says “BI Analyst” but asks only for a bachelor’s and lists Excel, SAP and Power BI, expect dashboards, not models.

title: Skill Gap Analysis
text: This section conducts a skill gap analysis by comparing our team’s current set of skills with the market’s expectations. By comparing the top in-demand skills found in the dataset to the team’s profeciency levels, possible gaps in knowledge can be identfied. A proposed improvement plan can ensure that individual development will align with market demands.

title: Skill Gap Analysis
text: This section conducts a skill gap analysis by comparing our team’s current set of skills with the market’s expectations. By comparing the top in-demand skills found in the dataset to the team’s profeciency levels, possible gaps in knowledge can be identfied. A proposed improvement plan can ensure that individual development will align with market demands.

title: Skill Gap Analysis
text:
Creating a Skill Level DataFrame
When first listing IT-related skills, our team ranked our proficiency levels on the following numerical scale:
1 = Beginner
2 = Basic knowledge
3 = Intermediate
4 = Advanced
5 = Expert
Orignally, we thought relevant skills would include coding languages, machine learning, and cloud computing. The following heatmap helps you visualize which group members excelled in which of these skillsets.


Show the code
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

skills_data = {
    "Name": ["Meera", "Simon", "Mia", "Keily"],
    "Python": [3, 3, 2, 3],
    "SQL": [4, 2, 2, 2],
    "Machine Learning": [3, 5, 5, 3],
    "Cloud Computing": [3, 3, 5, 2]
}

df_skills = pd.DataFrame(skills_data)
df_skills.set_index("Name", inplace=True)
# print(df_skills)


# Heat map of skill level data frame
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
sns.heatmap(df_skills, annot=True, cmap="coolwarm", linewidths=0.5)
plt.title("Team Skill Levels Heatmap")
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.xlabel("Skills")
plt.show()









The differences in proficiencies amongst the skills and teammates reveals an opportunity for team collaboration to bridge skill gaps.

title: Skill Gap Analysis
section: Comparing Team Skills to Industry Requirements
text:
Comparing Team Skills to Industry Requirements
After completing the exploratory data visualizations, it became apparent that the skills we assumed would be in highest demand were not all found in the top 10 in-demand skills of the dataset. After updating our list of skills, using the same numerical scale to indicate proficiency levels, we were able to compare our teams skills to the industry’s demands.


Show the code
skills_data = {
    "Name": ["Meera", "Simon", "Mia", "Keily"],
    "Communication":[3,4,2,4],
    "Data Analysis":[4,4,3,3],
    "Management":[3,2,2,3],
    "SQL":[4, 2, 2, 2],
    "Problem Solving":[5,5,5,5],
    "Leadership":[4,5,4,4],
    "Computer Science":[1,2,3,1],
    "Operations":[1,1,4,2],
    "Project Management":[3,2,2,3],
    "Business Process":[3,3,3,3]
}

import pandas as pd
from collections import Counter

# Top 10 In-Demand Skills in Job Postings found in exploratory data tab
top_skills = ["Communication", "Data Analysis", "Management", "SQL", "Problem Solving", "Leadership", "Computer Science","Operations", "Project Management", "Business Process"]
job_skill_counts = Counter(top_skills)

# Compare with team skill levels
for skill in top_skills:
    if skill not in df_skills.columns:
        df_skills[skill] = 0  # Assume no knowledge in missing skills

df_skills = pd.DataFrame(skills_data)
df_skills.set_index("Name", inplace=True)
df_skills

# Heat map of skill level data frame

title: Skill Gap Analysis
text:
Improvement Plan Proposal
Now that we have compared our current skill proficiency levels to the industry requirements, we can identify areas of improvement. The following steps can be taken to begin the process of strengthening in-demand skills:
(1) Prioritizing our learning
With data to support the top in-demand skills, we can prioritize areas of development to align with our individual career aspirations.
(2) Finding courses or resources to help
To build confidence and experience, it is important to find available resources. Courses offered in the BU ABA master’s program align with current and projected in-demand skills. Additional certifications can be found to improve machine learning and AI-related qualifications.
(3) Using team collaboration to bridge skill gaps
The heat map shows a wide range of proficiencies amongst the team members. Each team member brings a unique academic and professional background to the table. When collaborating on a team project like this one, it can be an effective learning tool to not just divide work amongst team members, but to take the time and understand what and how others contributed.

title: Data Science & Business Analytics Trends in 2024
text:
The demand for data science, business analytics, and machine learning professionals has continued to grow as organizations increasingly rely on data-driven decision making. At the same time, rapid advances in artificial intelligence have transformed skill requirements and created new opportunities across industries.
Using the Lightcast job postings dataset, this project investigates labor market trends from 2024. Specifically, we examine the most in-demand skills, the growing importance of AI and machine learning expertise, the industries hiring the largest number of data professionals, and the overall career outlook for business analytics practitioners.

title: Data Science & Business Analytics Trends in 2024
text:
The demand for data science, business analytics, and machine learning professionals has continued to grow as organizations increasingly rely on data-driven decision making. At the same time, rapid advances in artificial intelligence have transformed skill requirements and created new opportunities across industries.
Using the Lightcast job postings dataset, this project investigates labor market trends from 2024. Specifically, we examine the most in-demand skills, the growing importance of AI and machine learning expertise, the industries hiring the largest number of data professionals, and the overall career outlook for business analytics practitioners.

title: Data Science & Business Analytics Trends in 2024
text:
Research Rationale
Understanding labor market trends is important for students and professionals preparing for careers in data science and business analytics. Analyzing job postings provides valuable insights into employer expectations, skill requirements, salary patterns, and geographic demand.
In addition to exploratory data analysis, this project incorporates findings from external sources such as OECD, Stanford HAI, Lightcast reports, and academic literature. By combining labor market data with existing research, the project provides a broader understanding of how artificial intelligence is reshaping workforce demand and influencing the future of analytics-related careers.

title: Data Science & Business Analytics Trends in 2024
text:
Data Analysis
To begin data exploration and analysis, the dataset was put through comprehensive data cleaning. The second research question could then be properly examined. By finding the most frequently observed AI-related terms in the dataset, the data revealed the increase in AI-related language. The current job market demand is seen to have a combination of traditional AI/ML concepts alongside newer generative AI terms.

title: Data Science & Business Analytics Trends in 2024
text:
EDA Analysis

Chart 1: Job Postings by Industry
Why: Identifies where hiring demand is concentrated, helping job seekers know which industries to target.
Insights: Unclassified Industry dominates, followed by Custom Computer Programming Services and Administrative Management. This suggests strong demand in tech and business services sectors.


Chart 2: Salary Distribution by Industry
Why: Reveals not just average pay but salary spread and outliers within each industry.
Insights: Salaries across most industries cluster between 100𝑘−200k. Offices of Certified Public Accountants shows the tightest range, while Employment Placement Agencies and Temporary Help Services have extreme outliers pushing past 400𝑘−500k, likely reflecting contract or senior placements. Computer Systems Design Services has the most data points concentrated at the lower end, suggesting a high volume of entry to mid-level roles.


Chart 3: Remote vs. On-site
Why: Remote work flexibility is a key factor for job seekers evaluating opportunities.
Insights: 78% of postings have no remote type specified ([NONE]), which likely means on-site or unspecified. Only 17.2% are fully remote, 3.12% hybrid, and 1.56% explicitly not remote. This suggests that true remote flexibility remains limited, and most employers either require in-person work or do not advertise remote options at all.


Chart 4: Top In-Demand Skills
Why: Directly answers our research question on what skills DS/BA/ML roles require most.
Insights: Communication and Data Analysis are the top two skills by a significant margin, followed by Management and SQL Python appears further down the list, alongside Tableau and Dashboard skills. This suggests employers value a blend of soft skills and technical tools, not just coding ability.


Chart 5: Top Job Titles
Why: Shows which roles are actually being hired for, giving a realistic picture of the job market.
Insights: Data Analysts dominate postings by a wide margin with over 8,000 listings, nearly triple the next category. Business Intelligence Analysts and Enterprise Architects follow, with many specialized analyst roles (Data Governance, Data Quality, Data Modelers) also appearing. This indicates strong and diverse demand across the data field.


Chart 6: Salary By Job Title (Top 10 Titles)
Why: Shows earning potential across the most common roles to help with career planning.
Insights: Data Analytics Engineers command the highest salaries with a median around $200k and a wide range, while Data Analysts sit at the lower end around $100k-$150k despite being the most posted role. Enterprise Architects and Oracle Cloud HCM Consultants also show strong earning potential with notable outliers. This suggests that specialization is rewarded significantly over generalist analyst roles.


Chart 7: Job Postings By State
Why: Geographic variation in job postings helps job seekers understand where to target their search and whether relocation could significantly expand opportunities.
Insights: Texas and California dominate by a wide margin, together accounting for a large share of all postings. Florida, Virgina, Illinois, and New York follow, suggesting that major tech and business hubs remain the primary hiring centers.

title: Data Science & Business Analytics Trends in 2024
text:
Skill Gap Analysis
The team compiled a list of technical skills that they believed would be in high demand prior to conducting exploratory analysis. Upon reviewing the EDA results, the list was updated to include the top 10 in-demand skills. The team used their academic and professional experience to assign each skill with a proficiency level. The comparison of individual proficiencies and job market demand revealed possible areas of improvement.

title: Data Science & Business Analytics Trends in 2024
text:
Advanced Visualizations

Top 10 Skills Ranked by Month (2024) Filter by Job Title - Bump Chart
Why This Chart?
A bump chart shows how skill rankings shift over time across five months (May–September 2024). Filtering with the dropdown shows which skills are consistently prioritized vs. situationally demanded for each job title. This is something a static bar chart cannot reveal.
Key Insights

Most job titles show significant rank volatility across the five months, suggesting that employer skill priorities shift frequently within a hiring cycle rather than remaining fixed.
The same skill can rank #1 in one month and drop to #5 or lower the next, indicating that no single skill dominates consistently for most roles.
Technical titles like Data Analysts tend to cluster around data-related skills (SQL, Data Analysis, Power BI) in the top ranks, while management-adjacent titles show more varied and unstable rankings.
The May–July period tends to show the most movement, while August–September rankings appear slightly more stable, possibly reflecting end-of-year hiring consolidation.

Implications for Job Seekers
Use the dropdown to find your target job title and identify which skills hold the top 3 ranks consistently, those are your must-haves. Skills that fluctuate between ranks 4–10 are strong differentiators but not dealbreakers. Prioritize stable top-ranked skills in your resume before adding situational ones.


Skills Landscape by Industry (Interactive) - Treemap
Why This Chart?
This interactive version allows job seekers to focus on a single industry at a time, reducing visual noise and enabling direct comparison of role and skill demand within a specific sector.
Key Insights

Management Consulting shows the broadest skill variety, with Databricks, ServiceNow, and Tealium appearing alongside standard tools. This reflects hybrid technical-business roles.
Computer Systems Design heavily favors Microsoft Access and Progress Reporting for Data Analysts, suggesting a more traditional enterprise analytics stack.
Public Accountants is the smallest sector but shows BigQuery and Capital Markets skills, indicating emerging data modernization in accounting firms.



Industry → Job Title → Skills Flow - Sankey
Why This Chart?
A Sankey diagram visualizes flow and volume simultaneously. The width of each band represents the number of job postings flowing from an industry through a job title into a required skill. This makes it easy to see where demand is concentrated across the entire hiring pipeline at once.
Key Insights

Data Analysts is the dominant role across all industries, accounting for the majority of flow through the diagram.
Power BI is the single most demanded skill across all industries and titles.
Computer Programming and Management Consulting drive the highest volume of postings overall.
Life Sciences appears as a skill in Computer Programming firms, suggesting biotech/pharma adjacent data roles are growing in that sector.

Implications for Job Seekers
Focus on Data Analyst roles as the primary entry point. These represent the largest share of available positions across every major industry.


Job Title Demand by Industry - Heatmap
Why This Chart?
A heatmap is ideal for showing a two-dimensional comparison. Here we cross job titles against industries, with color intensity representing the number of postings. Darker cells immediately draw attention to where demand is highest.
Key Insights

Data Analysts in Computer Programming is the single hottest combination with 531 postings. By far the most in-demand role-industry pairing.
Data Analysts dominate across every industry, confirming they are the most sought-after data role regardless of sector.
Data Modelers show surprisingly strong demand in Computer Programming (122) and Computer Systems Design (61), suggesting these industries need professionals who can architect data infrastructure.
Public Accountants shows near-zero demand for most titles, confirming it is a niche sector for data roles.
Management Consulting shows relatively balanced demand across titles compared to other industries, suggesting consultancies hire a broader range of data professionals.

Implications for Job Seekers
If you are targeting a Data Analyst role, Computer Programming and Management Consulting firms offer the most opportunities. If you specialize in Data Modeling, Computer Programming is your primary target market.

title: Data Science & Business Analytics Trends in 2024
text:
Text Mining & Classification
The text mining of the job descriptions in the dataset was used to explore the difference between ML/DS-intensive roles and traditional BI roles. Supervised classification models then set out to predict whether a BI role is ML/DS-intensive using structured features. A logistic regression model and random forest model were compared using confusion matrices. Salary band and degree requirements were determined to be signifiers of true data-science roles.

title: Data Science & Business Analytics Trends in 2024
text:
KMeans Clustering Analysis
The unsupervised learning method was generated to identify hidden patterns amongst the dataset. The clusters produced by the model could be compared to NAICS industries as a way to identify possible classifications.

title: KMeans Clustering Analysis
text:
Introduction
This section applies KMeans clustering to identify hidden patterns among job postings. Since KMeans is an unsupervised learning method, it does not use a target variable. Instead, job postings are grouped based on salary and experience requirements.
NAICS industry classification is used as a reference label to compare whether the clusters align with existing industry categories.


Load Data


Code
import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.io as pio

from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA

Feature Selection
The KMeans model uses salary and experience variables because these features help describe job market segments from a job seeker perspective.


Code
features = [
    "SALARY_FROM",
    "SALARY_TO",
    "MIN_YEARS_EXPERIENCE",
    "MAX_YEARS_EXPERIENCE"
]

cluster_df = df[features].copy()
cluster_df = cluster_df.dropna()

Standardization
KMeans is distance-based, so the selected numerical features must be standardized before clustering.


Code
scaler = StandardScaler()
scaled_data = scaler.fit_transform(cluster_df)




KMeans Clustering


Code
kmeans = KMeans(
    n_clusters=4,
    random_state=42,
    n_init=10
)

cluster_df["Cluster"] = kmeans.fit_predict(scaled_data)

cluster_df.head()

Cluster Distribution


Code
cluster_counts = cluster_df["Cluster"].value_counts().reset_index()
cluster_counts.columns = ["Cluster", "Count"]
cluster_counts = cluster_counts.sort_values("Cluster")

cluster_counts["Cluster_Label"] = cluster_counts["Cluster"].map({
    0: "Cluster 0: Entry-Level Jobs",
    1: "Cluster 1: Mid-Level Jobs",
    2: "Cluster 2: Experienced Jobs",
    3: "Cluster 3: High Salary Jobs"
})

fig = px.bar(
    cluster_counts,
    x="Cluster",
    y="Count",
    color="Cluster_Label",
    title="Distribution of Job Postings by KMeans Cluster",
    text="Count"
)

fig.update_layout(
    xaxis_title="KMeans Cluster",
    yaxis_title="Number of Job Postings",
    height=500,
    xaxis=dict(
        tickmode="array",
        tickvals=[0, 1, 2, 3],
        ticktext=["0", "1", "2", "3"]
    ),
    legend_title_text="Cluster Meaning"
)

fig.show()


                                                





The clustering results indicate that most job postings belong to Cluster 1 and Cluster 2, suggesting that the labor market is dominated by medium- to high-demand positions. Cluster 0 represents a relatively small segment of postings, while Cluster 3 captures a specialized group with different salary and experience characteristics.

Cluster and Industry Visualization


Code
fig = px.bar(
    top_industry_by_cluster,
    x="Count",
    y="NAICS2_NAME",
    color="Cluster",
    orientation="h",
    title="Top NAICS Industries Within Each KMeans Cluster"

    xaxis_title="Number of Job Postings",
    yaxis_title="NAICS Industry",
    height=700
)

PCA Visualization of Clusters
PCA is used to reduce the standardized features into two dimensions so the clusters can be visualized.


Code
pca = PCA(n_components=2)
pca_components = pca.fit_transform(scaled_data)

cluster_df["PCA1"] = pca_components[:, 0]
cluster_df["PCA2"] = pca_components[:, 1]
cluster_df["Cluster_Label"] = cluster_df["Cluster"].map({
    0: "Entry-Level Jobs",
    1: "Mid-Level Jobs",
    2: "Experienced Jobs",
    3: "High Salary Jobs"
})

fig = px.scatter(
    cluster_df,
    x="PCA1",
    y="PCA2",
    color="Cluster_Label",
    title="KMeans Clusters Visualized with PCA",
    hover_data=["NAICS2_NAME"]
)

fig.add_vline(
    x=0,
    line_width=2,
    line_dash="dash",
    line_color="gray"
)

fig.add_hline(
    y=0,
    line_width=2,
    line_dash="dash",
    line_color="gray"
)

fig.update_layout(
    xaxis_title="PCA Component 1",
    yaxis_title="PCA Component 2",
    height=600,
    legend_title_text="Cluster"
)

fig.show()


                                                





The PCA plot shows that the four clusters are reasonably separated after dimensionality reduction, suggesting that KMeans identified meaningful group structures.


Interpretation
The KMeans model groups job postings based on salary and experience requirements. The NAICS industry labels are used as reference labels to help interpret whether clusters are associated with specific industry categories.
This analysis suggests that jobs with similar salary and experience profiles can be grouped into meaningful labor market segments. Comparing clusters with NAICS industries helps connect the unsupervised model results to real-world industry classifications.
These findings demonstrate that salary and experience variables alone can reveal meaningful labor market segments using unsupervised learning.


Implications for Job Seekers
For job seekers, clustering can help identify different types of career opportunities. Some clusters may represent lower-experience or entry-level roles, while others may represent higher-salary or more experienced positions.
Understanding these clusters can help students and professionals compare career paths, evaluate salary expectations, and identify which industries may offer stronger opportunities for data science, business analytics, and machine learning roles.
